parts.content: Given that $M^{2} - 6M + kI = O$, find $k$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate $M^2$: $M^{2} = \begin{pmatrix} 7 & -6 \\ -18 & 19 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate $6M$: $6M = \begin{pmatrix} 12 & -6 \\ -18 & 24 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute into the given equation: $\begin{pmatrix} 7 & -6 \\ -18 & 19 \end{pmatrix} - \begin{pmatrix} 12 & -6 \\ -18 & 24 \end{pmatrix} + k\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Simplify the matrix expression: $\begin{pmatrix} -5 & 0 \\ 0 & -5 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} k & 0 \\ 0 & k \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Form an equation for $k$: $-5 + k = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- State the final value: $k = 5$ <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

parts.content: Find the characteristic polynomial $p(\lambda)=\det(M - \lambda I)$.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute into the determinant formula for the characteristic polynomial: $\det(M - \lambda I) = \begin{vmatrix} 2-\lambda & -1 \\ -3 & 4-\lambda \end{vmatrix}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Expand the determinant: $(2-\lambda)(4-\lambda) - (-1)(-3)$ 

- Simplify to the final expression: $\lambda^{2} - 6\lambda + 5$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Write down the eigenvalues of $M$.

- Solve the quadratic equation to determine the eigenvalues: $\lambda = 1$ and $\lambda = 5$ <marks>A1A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the concept
steps.content:
In IB HL Mathematics, specifically under **Topic 1.15 (Eigenvalues and eigenvectors)**, we study how to diagonalize a matrix. 

When a matrix $M$ is diagonalizable, we can represent it using a diagonal matrix $D$ and a transition matrix $P$ (which consists of the eigenvectors of $M$). This relationship is a fundamental definition you are expected to recall directly.

highlights.text: Write down an expression for D in terms of P and M.

steps.title: Recall the formula
steps.content:
The standard identity used to find the diagonal matrix $D$ involves pre-multiplying the original matrix $M$ by the inverse of $P$ and post-multiplying by $P$. 

Directly from the definition of diagonalization:

$$D = P^{-1}MP$$

steps.title: State the final expression
steps.content:
Since the question is worth 1 mark and uses the command term "Write down", you do not need to show a derivation. Simply state the expression clearly.

$$D = P^{-1}MP$$

steps.title: State the starting equation
steps.content:
To find the expression for $D$, we start with the similarity transformation provided in the method description:

$$M = PDP^{-1}$$

This formula relates a matrix $M$ to its diagonal matrix $D$ and the matrix of eigenvectors $P$. Our goal is to rearrange this algebraically to isolate $D$.

steps.title: Post-multiply by $P$
steps.content:
In matrix algebra, order matters because multiplication is not commutative. To begin isolating $D$, we first eliminate $P^{-1}$ by **post-multiplying** (multiplying on the right) both sides of the equation by $P$:

$$\begin{aligned} M P &= (PDP^{-1}) P \\ MP &= PD(P^{-1}P) \end{aligned}$$

Since any matrix multiplied by its inverse results in the identity matrix ($P^{-1}P = I$), the equation simplifies to:

$$MP = PD$$

steps.title: Pre-multiply by $P^{-1}$
steps.content:
Next, we need to remove the $P$ matrix from the left side of $D$. We do this by **pre-multiplying** (multiplying on the left) both sides of the equation by $P^{-1}$:

$$\begin{aligned} P^{-1}(MP) &= P^{-1}(PD) \\ P^{-1}MP &= (P^{-1}P)D \end{aligned}$$

Using the identity property ($P^{-1}P = I$) again, we are left with $D$ on its own:

$$P^{-1}MP = D$$

steps.title: Final expression for $D$
steps.content:
By rearranging the terms, we have successfully isolated the diagonal matrix. The final expression is:

$$D = P^{-1}MP$$

This is a standard identity used in **Topic 1: Number and Algebra** (specifically AHL 1.15) to diagonalize matrices.

questions.specification:
This question will investigate the solution to a coupled system of differential equations when there is only one eigenvalue.

Solve the coupled system of differential equations

$$
\dot{x} = 3x + y
$$

$$
\dot{y} = -x + y
$$

A general solution to the coupled system of differential equations is given by

$$
\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = A\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}e^{2t} + B\begin{pmatrix} t \\ -t + 1 \end{pmatrix}e^{2t}
$$

parts.content:
Show that the matrix

$$
\begin{pmatrix}
3 & 1 \\
-1 & 1
\end{pmatrix}
$$

has a repeated eigenvalue (i.e., only one distinct eigenvalue). Find this eigenvalue and an associated eigenvector.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the characteristic polynomial: $\begin{vmatrix} 3 - \lambda & 1 \\ -1 & 1 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = 0 \Rightarrow (3 - \lambda)(1 - \lambda) + 1 = 0$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

- Solve for $\lambda$: $\lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 4 = 0 \Rightarrow (\lambda - 2)^2 = 0$ <marks>A1 A1</marks>

- State the result: So only one solution $\lambda = 2$ <marks>AG A1</marks>

- Solve for the eigenvector: $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -1 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} p \\ q \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \Rightarrow p + q = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- State the eigenvector: So an eigenvector is $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

7 marks total

parts.content:
Using your eigenvalue and eigenvector, verify that

$$
\begin{pmatrix}
x \\
y
\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}
1 \\
-1
\end{pmatrix}e^{2t}
$$

is a solution to the above system.
parts.markscheme:
- Verify matrix multiplication: $\begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} = 2\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute into system: $\begin{pmatrix} 3x + y \\ -x + y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}e^{2t} = 2\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}e^{2t}$ <marks>A1 A1</marks>

- Differentiate the given solution: $\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}e^{2t} \Rightarrow \begin{pmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}2e^{2t}$ <marks>M1 A1</marks>

- Conclude that $\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}e^{2t}$ is a solution. <marks>AG</marks>

5 marks total

parts.content:
Verify that

$$
\begin{pmatrix}
x \\
y
\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}
t \\
-t + 1
\end{pmatrix}e^{2t}
$$

is also a solution.
If initially at $t = 0$, $x = 20$, $y = 10$, find the particular solution.

3 marks total

parts.content: Find the equilibrium point for this system.
parts.markscheme: - Set derivatives to zero: $$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} = 0 &\Rightarrow x + 2y - 50 = 0 \\ \dot{y} = 0 &\Rightarrow 2x + y - 40 = 0 \end{aligned}$$ <marks>M1</marks>  - Solve for $x$ and $y$: $\Rightarrow x = 10, \, y = 20$ <marks>A1</marks>  2 marks total

steps.title: Define the equilibrium conditions
steps.content:
In the context of differential equations, an **equilibrium point** (or steady state) occurs when the variables are no longer changing over time. This means the rates of change, represented by $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$, must both be equal to zero.

This concept is a key part of **Topic 5: Calculus (AHL 5.17)** regarding coupled differential equations. To find this point, we start by setting:
$$\dot{x} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \dot{y} = 0$$

steps.title: Set up the linear system
steps.content:
Using the equations provided in the question, we substitute zero for the derivatives to create a system of two linear equations:

$$\begin{aligned} 1) \quad x + 2y - 50 &= 0 \\ 2) \quad 2x + y - 40 &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

This matches the first step in the markscheme to earn the first method mark (**M1**).

steps.title: Solve using substitution
steps.content:
To solve this algebraically, we can isolate $x$ in the first equation and substitute it into the second:

From equation (1):
$$x = 50 - 2y$$

Now, substitute this expression for $x$ into equation (2):
$$\begin{aligned} 2(50 - 2y) + y - 40 &= 0 \\ 100 - 4y + y - 40 &= 0 \\ 60 - 3y &= 0 \\ 3y &= 60 \\ y &= 20 \end{aligned}$$

Finally, find $x$ by plugging $y = 20$ back into our expression for $x$:
$$x = 50 - 2(20) = 10$$

steps.title: Verify with technology
steps.content: In an IB exam, you can use your Graphic Display Calculator (GDC) to solve systems of linear equations quickly. This is a great way to double-check your algebraic work.

gdc.instructions: Go to the Equation/Solver menu. Select 'Simultaneous'. Set the number of unknowns to 2. Enter the coefficients: a1=1, b1=2, c1=50 and a2=2, b2=1, c2=40. Press 'Solve'.

ti84.instructions: Press [APPS], select 'PlySmlt2', then 'Simult Eqn Solver'. Set equations to 2 and unknowns to 2. Enter the coefficients into the matrix: [[1, 2, 50], [2, 1, 40]]. Press [GRAPH] (SOLVE).

desmos.instructions: Type the two equations into the input bars. The intersection of the two lines is the solution.

calculator.description: Solve the system of linear equations x + 2y = 50 and 2x + y = 40.

steps.title: State the equilibrium point
steps.content:
The equilibrium point for the symbiotic coral populations is:
$$(10, 20)$$

This means that when there are 10 units of population $x$ and 20 units of population $y$, the system is in a state where neither population is growing or shrinking (**A1**).

steps.title: Define the Equilibrium Point
steps.content:
An equilibrium point in a system of differential equations occurs when the rates of change for all variables are zero. This means the population sizes are not changing over time. 

To find this point, we must set the derivatives $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$ to zero:

$$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= 0 \\ \dot{y} &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Rearrange for the Solver
steps.content:
To use the **Simultaneous Equation Solver** on your GDC, the equations need to be in the standard form $ax + by = c$. 

We set our expressions equal to zero and move the constants to the right side:

1.  $x + 2y - 50 = 0 \Rightarrow x + 2y = 50$
2.  $2x + y - 40 = 0 \Rightarrow 2x + y = 40$

This gives us a system of two linear equations with two unknowns ($x$ and $y$). This technique is covered in **Topic 1.8: Use of technology to solve systems of linear equations**.

steps.title: Solve Using GDC
steps.content:
Now, we input the coefficients into the GDC to solve for $x$ and $y$. Our coefficients for the matrix or solver are:

$$\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 50 \\ 2 & 1 & 40 \end{pmatrix}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the 'EQUA' (Equation) menu.
2. Select 'Simultaneous' (F1).
3. Select '2' unknowns (F1).
4. Enter the coefficients into the matrix:
   Row 1: [1, 2, 50]
   Row 2: [2, 1, 40]
5. Press 'SOLVE' (F1).

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [APPS] and select 'PlySmlt2'.
2. Select '2: Simultaneous Eqn Solver'.
3. Set equations to '2' and unknowns to '2'. Press [NEXT] (Graph button).
4. Enter the coefficients: 
   Row 1: 1, 2, 50
   Row 2: 2, 1, 40
5. Press [SOLVE] (Graph button).

desmos.instructions:
In the Matrix Calculator:
1. Create Matrix A: [[1, 2], [2, 1]]
2. Create Matrix B: [[50], [40]]
3. Calculate A⁻¹B to find the values of x and y.

calculator.description: Solve the system of linear equations using the GDC.

steps.title: State the Equilibrium Point
steps.content:
The calculator provides the solution for our system:

$$x = 10, \, y = 20$$

Therefore, the equilibrium point for the symbiotic coral populations is $(10, 20)$. This means that if there are 10 of type $x$ and 20 of type $y$, the populations will remain constant over time.

steps.title: Define Equilibrium
steps.content:
In a system of differential equations, an **equilibrium point** occurs when the variables are no longer changing over time. This means the rates of change, $\dot{x}$ and $\dot{y}$, must both be equal to zero.

We start by setting our two equations to zero:

$$\begin{aligned} \dot{x} &= 0 \Rightarrow x + 2y - 50 = 0 \\ \dot{y} &= 0 \Rightarrow 2x + y - 40 = 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Rearrange First Equation
steps.content:
To use the **Graphical Intersection Method**, we need to write our equations in the form $y = f(x)$ so we can enter them into a graphing calculator.

Let's rearrange the first equilibrium equation:

$$\begin{aligned} x + 2y - 50 &= 0 \\ 2y &= 50 - x \\ y &= 25 - 0.5x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Rearrange Second Equation
steps.content:
Now, we do the same for the second equilibrium equation to isolate $y$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2x + y - 40 &= 0 \\ y &= 40 - 2x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the Intersection
steps.content: Now that we have two functions, $y_1 = 25 - 0.5x$ and $y_2 = 40 - 2x$, we can plot them and find where they cross. This intersection point is the solution that satisfies both equations simultaneously.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Graphing menu.
2. Enter f1(x) = 25 - 0.5x.
3. Enter f2(x) = 40 - 2x.
4. Draw the graph and use the 'Intersection' tool (usually under Analyze Graph or G-Solv).

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = 25 - 0.5X.
3. Enter Y2 = 40 - 2X.
4. Press [GRAPH]. (Adjust [WINDOW] if needed, e.g., Xmax=50, Ymax=50).
5. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) and select '5: intersect'.
6. Press [ENTER] for the first curve, [ENTER] for the second curve, and [ENTER] to guess.

desmos.instructions:
1. Type y = 25 - 0.5x into the first expression box.
2. Type y = 40 - 2x into the second expression box.
3. Click on the point where the two lines cross to see the coordinates.

calculator.description: Find the intersection point of the two population lines.

steps.title: State Equilibrium Point
steps.content:
Using the graphing tool, we find that the lines intersect at:

$$x = 10, \, y = 20$$

This means the equilibrium point for the coral populations is $(10, 20)$. This corresponds to the first mark for setting equations to zero and the second mark for the correct values.

This technique relates to **Topic 2.4: Features of a graph**, specifically finding intersection points of different functions.

parts.content: If initially $x = 100$ and $y = 50$, use Euler's method with a time increment of 0.1 to find an approximation for the values of $x$ and $y$ when $t = 1$.
parts.markscheme: - Using Euler's method formulas: $$\begin{aligned} x_{n+1} &= x_n + 0.1(x_n + 2y_n - 50) \\ y_{n+1} &= y_n + 0.1(2x_n + y_n - 40) \\ t_{n+1} &= t_n + 0.1 \end{aligned}$$ <marks>M1</marks>  - Calculate values at $t=1$: $x(1) \approx 848, \, y(1) \approx 837$ (3 s.f.) <marks>A1A1</marks>  3 marks total

steps.title: Define Euler's Method Formulas
steps.content:
To approximate the values of $x$ and $y$ in a coupled system of differential equations, we use the iterative Euler's method formulas:

$$\begin{aligned} x_{n+1} &= x_n + h \cdot \dot{x}_n \\ y_{n+1} &= y_n + h \cdot \dot{y}_n \\ t_{n+1} &= t_n + h \end{aligned}$$

Substituting the given differential equations $\dot{x} = x + 2y - 50$ and $\dot{y} = 2x + y - 40$ with a step size of $h = 0.1$, we get:

$$\begin{aligned} x_{n+1} &= x_n + 0.1(x_n + 2y_n - 50) \\ y_{n+1} &= y_n + 0.1(2x_n + y_n - 40) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify Initial Conditions
steps.content:
We start at $t = 0$ with the following initial populations:

- $x_0 = 100$
- $y_0 = 50$
- $h = 0.1$

Since we want to find the values at $t = 1$, we need to perform 10 iterations (since $10 \times 0.1 = 1$).

steps.title: Calculate First Iteration (t=0.1)
steps.content:
Now we substitute $x_0 = 100$ and $y_0 = 50$ into our formulas to find the values at $t = 0.1$:

$$\begin{aligned} x_1 &= 100 + 0.1(100 + 2(50) - 50) \\ &= 100 + 0.1(150) = 115 \\ y_1 &= 50 + 0.1(2(100) + 50 - 40) \\ &= 50 + 0.1(210) = 71 \end{aligned}$$

So at $t = 0.1$, our approximation is $(115, 71)$.

steps.title: Complete Iterations Using Technology
steps.content:
Continuing this manual iterative substitution for 10 steps (until $t = 1.0$) gives the following results:

- $t=0.3: x_3 = 160.69, y_3 = 129.95$
- $t=0.4: x_4 \approx 191.8, y_4 \approx 171.1$
- ...and so on.

After 10 steps, we find:
$$x(1) \approx 847.61...$$
$$y(1) \approx 836.69...$$

gdc.instructions:
Create a recursive formula for two variables. 
Set Initial conditions: u1=100, v1=50.
Set formulas: 
an+1 = an + 0.1*(an + 2*bn - 50)
bn+1 = bn + 0.1*(2*an + bn - 40)
Look for the 11th term (which represents n=10 iterations).

ti84.instructions: Use the 'Sequence' mode in the Y= menu. Set nMin=0, u(n)=u(n-1)+0.1(u(n-1)+2v(n-1)-50), and v(n)=v(n-1)+0.1(2u(n-1)+v(n-1)-40). Set u(nMin)=100 and v(nMin)=50. Go to the table and find n=10.

desmos.instructions: While Desmos isn't ideal for manual recursion like a GDC, you can verify the values using a list or a simple loop calculation.

calculator.description: To avoid manual error, you can use the recursive sequence feature or a spreadsheet-like entry on your GDC.

steps.title: State Final Rounded Answer
steps.content:
Finally, we round our results to 3 significant figures as required by standard IB conventions (unless otherwise specified).

$$x(1) \approx 848$$
$$y(1) \approx 837$$

steps.title: Establish the Euler Formulas
steps.content:
Euler's method for a coupled system uses the gradient (derivative) at the current point to estimate the value at the next point. Given the step size $h = 0.1$, the initial values, and the differential equations, we define the iterative process as follows:

$$\begin{aligned} t_{n+1} &= t_n + 0.1 \\ x_{n+1} &= x_n + 0.1(x_n + 2y_n - 50) \\ y_{n+1} &= y_n + 0.1(2x_n + y_n - 40) \end{aligned}$$

This is consistent with the general Euler formula for coupled systems: 

$$x_{n+1} = x_n + h \cdot \dot{x}(x_n, y_n)$$
$$y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot \dot{y}(x_n, y_n)$$

steps.title: Configure GDC Spreadsheet
steps.content:
To avoid manual calculation errors across 10 steps, we use the Spreadsheet application on the GDC. We will set up three columns for $t$, $x$, and $y$. 

1.  In **Row 1**, enter the initial conditions: $t=0, x=100, y=50$.
2.  In **Row 2**, enter the formulas to reference the cells above:
    *   For $t$: `= A1 + 0.1`
    *   For $x$: `= B1 + 0.1 * (B1 + 2 * C1 - 50)`
    *   For $y$: `= C1 + 0.1 * (2 * B1 + C1 - 40)`
3.  **Fill Down** these formulas until you reach $t = 1$. This requires 10 iterations (reaching Row 11).

gdc.instructions:
1. Open Spreadsheet. 
2. Col A: Start at 0, next cell `= a1 + 0.1`. 
3. Col B: Start at 100, next cell `= b1 + 0.1*(b1 + 2*c1 - 50)`. 
4. Col C: Start at 50, next cell `= c1 + 0.1*(2*b1 + c1 - 40)`. 
5. Use 'Fill' command to copy Row 2 down to Row 11.

ti84.instructions:
The TI-84 doesn't have a full spreadsheet, but you can use Lists. 
1. Go to [STAT] -> Edit. 
2. In L1 (for t), enter 0, 0.1, ..., 1. 
3. In L2 (for x) and L3 (for y), you must calculate row-by-row manually or use a small program. 
Iter 1: 100 + 0.1(100+2*50-50) = 115 and 50 + 0.1(2*100+50-40) = 71.
Repeat until t=1.

desmos.instructions:
Use a table. 
1. Add a table with columns t, x, y. 
2. Set initial values in row 1. 
3. Since Desmos doesn't support recursive tables easily, use a script or compute sequentially: x1 = 115, y1 = 71, etc.

calculator.description: Calculate Euler's method iterations for the coupled system.

steps.title: Identify Values at t = 1
steps.content:
After 10 iterations, we look at the row where $t = 1$ (usually row 11 in the spreadsheet). The calculator provides the following approximate values:

$$x(1) \approx 847.611...$$
$$y(1) \approx 836.690...$$

Rounding these to 3 significant figures as required by IB standards, we get:

$$x(1) \approx 848$$
$$y(1) \approx 837$$

parts.content: Extend this method to conjecture the limit of the ratio $\frac{y}{x}$ as $t \to \infty$.
parts.markscheme: - By extending the table, conjecture that: $\lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{y}{x} = 1$ <marks>M1A1</marks>  2 marks total

parts.content: Show how using the substitution $X = x - 10, \, Y = y - 20$ transforms the system of differential equations into $$\begin{aligned} \dot{X} &= X + 2Y \\ \dot{Y} &= 2X + Y \end{aligned}$$
parts.markscheme: - State derivatives relation: $X = x - 10, \, Y = y - 20 \Rightarrow \dot{X} = \dot{x}, \, \dot{Y} = \dot{y}$ <marks>R1</marks>  - Substitute into original equations: $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{X} &= (X + 10) + 2(Y + 20) - 50 = X + 2Y \\ \dot{Y} &= 2(X + 10) + (Y + 20) - 40 = 2X + Y \end{aligned} $$ <marks>M1A1AG</marks>  3 marks total

parts.content: Solve this system of equations by the eigenvalue method and hence find the general solution for $\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix}$ of the original system.
parts.markscheme: - Find eigenvalues: $$ \begin{vmatrix} 1 - \lambda & 2 \\ 2 & 1 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = 0 \Rightarrow (1 - \lambda)^2 - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda = -1 \text{ or } 3 $$ <marks>M1A1A1</marks>  - For $\lambda = -1$: $$ \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} p \\ q \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \Rightarrow q = -p $$ An eigenvector is $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$.  - For $\lambda = 3$: $$ \begin{pmatrix} -2 & 2 \\ 2 & -2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} p \\ q \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} \Rightarrow q = p $$ An eigenvector is $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$. <marks>M1A1A1</marks>  - General solution: $$ \begin{pmatrix} X \\ Y \end{pmatrix} = A e^{-t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + B e^{3t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} $$ $$ \Rightarrow \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = A e^{-t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + B e^{3t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 10 \\ 20 \end{pmatrix} $$ <marks>A1A1</marks>  8 marks total

parts.content: Find the particular solution to the original system, given the initial conditions of part 2.
parts.markscheme: - Substitute $t=0, x=100, y=50$: $$ \begin{aligned} 100 &= A + B + 10 \\ 50 &= -A + B + 20 \end{aligned} \Rightarrow A = 30, \, B = 60 $$ <marks>M1</marks>  - Write particular solution: $$ \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = 30 e^{-t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + 60 e^{3t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 10 \\ 20 \end{pmatrix} $$ <marks>A1</marks>  2 marks total

parts.content: Hence find the exact values of $x$ and $y$ when $t = 1$, giving the answers to 4 significant figures.
parts.markscheme: - Calculate values: $x(1) = 1226, \, y(1) = 1214$ (4 s.f.) <marks>A1A1</marks>  2 marks total

steps.title: Recall the general solution
steps.content:
From the previous parts of the investigation (using the eigenvector method), we have derived the general solution for the population of the two coral species. These analytical expressions for $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ are:

$$\begin{aligned} x(t) &= 60e^{3t} + 30e^{-t} + 10 \\ y(t) &= 60e^{3t} - 30e^{-t} + 20 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute the time value
steps.content:
We need to find the populations at $t = 1$. Substitute $t = 1$ into our general equations for $x$ and $y$:

$$\begin{aligned} x(1) &= 60e^{3(1)} + 30e^{-(1)} + 10 \\ y(1) &= 60e^{3(1)} - 30e^{-(1)} + 20 \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: exact values of x and y when t = 1

steps.title: Evaluate population values
steps.content:
Using your GDC, evaluate the exact expressions to find the population values. 

$$\begin{aligned} x(1) &= 60e^3 + 30e^{-1} + 10 \approx 1226.168599 \\ y(1) &= 60e^3 - 30e^{-1} + 20 \approx 1214.095832 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions:
Type the expressions directly into the calculation screen:
1. 60 * exp(3) + 30 * exp(-1) + 10
2. 60 * exp(3) - 30 * exp(-1) + 20

ti84.instructions:
Enter the following on the home screen:
1. 60 * e^(3) + 30 * e^(-1) + 10
2. 60 * e^(3) - 30 * e^(-1) + 20

desmos.instructions:
In separate rows, type:
1. x = 60e^3 + 30e^-1 + 10
2. y = 60e^3 - 30e^-1 + 20

calculator.description: Calculate the population values for x and y at t=1.

steps.title: Apply significant figures
steps.content:
The question asks for the answers to 4 significant figures. 

For $x$: $1226.168...$ rounds to $1226$.
For $y$: $1214.095...$ rounds to $1214$.

steps.title: State final answers
steps.content:
The populations after one decade are:

$$x(1) = 1226$$
$$y(1) = 1214$$

steps.title: Identify system and initial values
steps.content:
To find the values of $x$ and $y$ at $t = 1$, we first need to identify the coupled system of differential equations and the initial populations provided in the earlier parts of this investigation.

The system is:
$$\begin{cases} \dot{x} = x + 2y - 50 \\ \dot{y} = 2x + y - 40 \end{cases}$$

From the previous parts of the question, we use the initial conditions at $t=0$:
$$x(0) = 100, \quad y(0) = 50$$

steps.title: Input into GDC Solver
steps.content:
We will use the numerical differential equation solver on your graphing calculator. This tool calculates values step-by-step using methods like Euler's or Runge-Kutta, which is perfect for coupled systems that are difficult to solve by hand.

You need to set the graph mode to **Differential Equations** and enter the equations as functions of $x$, $y$, and $t$. Note that on many calculators, $x$ and $y$ are represented as $y_1$ and $y_2$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Open the Graphing application and change the graph type to 'Differential Equation'.
2. Enter $y1' = y1 + 2y2 - 50$ with $y1(0) = 100$.
3. Enter $y2' = 2y1 + y2 - 40$ with $y2(0) = 50$.
4. Use the table feature ([Ctrl][T]) to see the values at $t=1$.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MODE] and select 'DIFF EQUATIONS'.
2. Press [Y=].
3. Set $Y_1' = Y_1 + 2Y_2 - 50$ and $Y_2' = 2Y_1 + Y_2 - 40$.
4. Set initial conditions: $Y_{1i} = 100$ and $Y_{2i} = 50$.
5. Press [2nd][GRAPH] (TABLE) or [2nd][TRACE] (CALC) to find the values at $t=1$.

desmos.instructions:
1. Use the system of ODEs syntax: $x'(t) = x + 2y - 50$ and $y'(t) = 2x + y - 40$.
2. Set the initial points $(x_0, y_0) = (100, 50)$.
3. Look at the table of values generated for $t=1$.

calculator.description: Solve the system using numerical methods at t=1.

steps.title: State final values
steps.content:
Using the numerical solver, we find the following results at $t=1$:

$$\begin{aligned} x(1) &\approx 1226.168... \\ y(1) &\approx 1214.095... \end{aligned}$$

Rounding these to 4 significant figures as requested, we get:

$$x(1) = 1226, \quad y(1) = 1214$$

parts.content: Use part 6 to find limit of the ratio $\frac{y}{x}$ as $t \to \infty$.
parts.markscheme: - Identify dominant term: Dominant term is $60 e^{3t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$  - State limit: So $\lim_{t \to \infty} \frac{y}{x} = 1$ <marks>M1A1</marks>  2 marks total

parts.content: With the initial conditions as given in part 2, determine if the populations converge to the equilibrium values.
parts.markscheme: - State conclusion: The populations do not converge (unstable trajectory). <marks>R1</marks>  1 mark total

parts.content: If instead the initial conditions were given as $x = 20$ and $y = 10$, find the particular solution for $\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix}$ of the original system, in this case.
parts.markscheme: - Solve for constants: $$ \begin{aligned} 20 &= A + B + 10 \\ 10 &= -A + B + 20 \end{aligned} \Rightarrow A = 10, \, B = 0 $$ <marks>M1</marks>  - Write solution: $$ \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = 10 e^{-t} \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} 10 \\ 20 \end{pmatrix} $$ <marks>A1</marks>  2 marks total

parts.content: With the initial conditions as given in part 10, determine if the populations converge to the equilibrium values.
parts.markscheme: - Reason about convergence: As $e^{-t} \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$, the populations converge. <marks>R1A1</marks>  2 marks total

steps.title: Recall the solution form
steps.content:
From the previous parts of the investigation, the general solution for the populations $x$ and $y$ is expressed as a combination of a constant equilibrium value and exponential terms. The exponents are determined by the eigenvalues of the system, which are $\lambda_1 = -1$ and $\lambda_2 = 3$.

Given the specific initial conditions in part 10, the solution only contains the decaying exponential term associated with the negative eigenvalue. The general form for the population at time $t$ is:

$$\begin{aligned} x(t) &= 10 + A e^{-t} \\ y(t) &= 20 + B e^{-t} \end{aligned}$$

where 10 and 20 are the equilibrium populations.

steps.title: Evaluate the long-term limit
steps.content:
To determine if the populations converge, we evaluate the limit as time $t$ approaches infinity ($t \to \infty$). 

We know that for any negative exponent, the term approaches zero as the variable grows:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} e^{-t} = 0$$

As $t$ becomes very large, the parts of the population that vary over time ($A e^{-t}$ and $B e^{-t}$) disappear.

gdc.instructions: Graph the function f(x) = e^(-x). Use the trace tool to observe that as x increases, f(x) approaches the x-axis (y = 0).

ti84.instructions: Press [Y=] and enter Y1 = e^(-X). Press [GRAPH]. Use [TRACE] and scroll to the right to see the Y-value decrease toward zero.

desmos.instructions: Type y = e^(-x) into the expression bar. Observe the horizontal asymptote at y = 0 as x increases.

calculator.description: You can verify the behavior of the exponential decay function on your calculator to see it approaching zero.

steps.title: Conclude on convergence
steps.content:
Because the time-dependent terms vanish, the populations will eventually reach a steady state. Specifically, the populations converge to the equilibrium values:

$$\begin{aligned} x &\to 10 \\ y &\to 20 \end{aligned}$$

Since the populations approach fixed constant values, we conclude that **the populations do converge** to the equilibrium values.

steps.title: Identify eigenvalues and equilibrium
steps.content:
To determine the long-term behavior of the coral populations, we first look at the eigenvalues of the system. From the previous parts, we found the eigenvalues to be:

$$\lambda_1 = 3 \quad \text{and} \quad \lambda_2 = -1$$

and the equilibrium point to be $(10, 20)$. In a coupled system, the solution is a combination of exponential terms related to these eigenvalues:

$$\begin{pmatrix} x(t) - 10 \\ y(t) - 20 \end{pmatrix} = c_1 e^{3t} \mathbf{v}_1 + c_2 e^{-t} \mathbf{v}_2$$

where $\mathbf{v}_1$ and $\mathbf{v}_2$ are the eigenvectors. This system is technically unstable because $\lambda_1 = 3 > 0$, which would normally cause the population to grow away from equilibrium.

steps.title: Analyze initial conditions
steps.content:
The question specifies we use the initial conditions from the previous part. In those conditions, the state of the system lies exactly on the line of the eigenvector associated with the negative eigenvalue ($\lambda_2 = -1$). 

Because the initial state is on this stable eigenvector, the coefficient for the unstable growth term is zero ($c_1 = 0$). This means the general solution simplifies to:

$$\begin{pmatrix} x(t) - 10 \\ y(t) - 20 \end{pmatrix} = c_2 e^{-t} \mathbf{v}_2$$

steps.title: Evaluate the limit
steps.content:
Now we examine what happens as time $t$ increases. For any value of $c_2$, we look at the limit of the exponential term:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} e^{-t} = 0$$

As $t \to \infty$, the term $c_2 e^{-t} \mathbf{v}_2$ approaches the zero vector. This means the displacement from the equilibrium point disappears over time.

gdc.instructions: Graph y = e^(-x) and use the trace function to see the value as x becomes large.

ti84.instructions: Go to Y= and enter Y1 = e^(-X). Press TABLE (2nd + GRAPH) to see values as X increases.

desmos.instructions: Plot y = e^(-x) and observe the horizontal asymptote at y = 0.

calculator.description: Observe how e^(-t) behaves as t increases.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
Since the only remaining part of the solution decays to zero, the populations $x$ and $y$ will move closer and closer to their equilibrium values of $10$ and $20$.

Therefore, the populations **converge** to the equilibrium values.

questions.specification:
A pharmacologist is modelling the concentration of a medication in a patient's bloodstream following an intramuscular injection. The variable $x$ represents the concentration of the medication in milligrams per litre ($\text{mg L}^{-1}$) at time $t$ hours after the injection.

The initial concentration in the bloodstream is $0 \, \text{mg L}^{-1}$ and the rate of increase of the concentration is initially $5 \, \text{mg L}^{-1} \text{hr}^{-1}$.

parts.content:
Show that the system of coupled first order differential equations:
$$ \frac{dx}{dt} = y, \quad \frac{dy}{dt} = -6x - 5y $$
can be written as the second order differential equation:
$$ \frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + 5\frac{dx}{dt} + 6x = 0 $$
parts.markscheme:
- Differentiate the first equation with respect to $t$:
  $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = \frac{dy}{dt}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute $-6x - 5y$ for $\frac{dy}{dt}$:
  $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -6x - 5y$ 
  Substitute $\frac{dx}{dt}$ for $y$:
  $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} = -6x - 5\frac{dx}{dt}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Rearrange to the required form:
  $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + 5\frac{dx}{dt} + 6x = 0$ <marks>AG</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content: Find the eigenvalues of the system of coupled first order equations given in part 1.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the relevant matrix:
  $A = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>(A1)</marks>

- Set up the characteristic equation $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$:
  $-\lambda(-5-\lambda) + 6 = 0$ OR $\lambda^2 + 5\lambda + 6 = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Solve for $\lambda$:
  $(\lambda + 2)(\lambda + 3) = 0$
  $\lambda = -2, -3$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Hence find the exact solution for the concentration $x$ at time $t$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the general solution:
  $x = Ae^{-2t} + Be^{-3t}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Differentiate to find $\frac{dx}{dt}$:
  $\frac{dx}{dt} = -2Ae^{-2t} - 3Be^{-3t}$

- Apply initial conditions ($x=0$ and $\frac{dx}{dt}=5$ at $t=0$):
  $0 = A + B$
  $5 = -2A - 3B$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Solve for $A$ and $B$:
  $A = 5, B = -5$

- State the exact solution:
  $x = 5e^{-2t} - 5e^{-3t}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Sketch the graph of $x$ against $t$, labelling the coordinates of the point where the concentration is at its maximum.
parts.markscheme:
- Sketch graph showing correct shape: starts at $(0,0)$, reaches a peak, and then approaches the $t$-axis as an asymptote. <marks>A1</marks>

- Find maximum point coordinates:
  $\frac{dx}{dt} = -10e^{-2t} + 15e^{-3t} = 0 \implies e^t = 1.5 \implies t = \ln(1.5) \approx 0.405$
  $x(\ln 1.5) = 5\left(\frac{1}{1.5}\right)^2 - 5\left(\frac{1}{1.5}\right)^3 = \frac{20}{27} \approx 0.741$
  Maximum at $(\ln 1.5, \frac{20}{27})$ or $(0.405, 0.741)$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

parts.content:
The medication is considered to be therapeutically effective only when the concentration in the bloodstream is at least $0.5 \, \text{mg L}^{-1}$.

Calculate the total duration of time for which the medication is effective.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the condition $x = 0.5$:
  $5e^{-2t} - 5e^{-3t} = 0.5$ OR $e^{-2t} - e^{-3t} = 0.1$ <marks>(M1)</marks>

- Find the intersection points (using GDC or algebraic solver):
  $t_1 \approx 0.1288...$ and $t_2 \approx 0.8911...$ <marks>(A1)</marks>

- Calculate the difference:
  $0.8911... - 0.1288... = 0.7623...$
  Duration = $0.762$ hours <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Suggest one limitation of using this model to predict the drug concentration over a long period of time.
parts.markscheme:
- State a valid limitation with reference to the context. <marks>R1</marks>

  - The model assumes a constant rate of elimination which may change as the patient's metabolism adjusts.
  - Biological factors such as kidney or liver function are not constant over long periods.
  - Physical activity could change the rate of absorption from the muscle into the blood.

parts.content: Given the matrix $B = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ -1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$, find the values of the real number $k$ for which $\det(B - kJ) = 0$ where $J = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$.
parts.markscheme:
- **Identify** the matrices:
  $B = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ -1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ and $J = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$

- **Form** the matrix $B - kJ$:
  $B - kJ = \begin{pmatrix} 3-k & 2 \\ -1 & -k \end{pmatrix}$

- **Calculate** the determinant of $B - kJ$:
  $\det(B - kJ) = (3-k)(-k) - (2)(-1) = k^2 - 3k + 2$ <marks>M1</marks>

- **Set** the determinant equal to zero:
  $k^2 - 3k + 2 = 0$ <marks>A1</marks>

- **Solve** the quadratic equation:
  $(k-1)(k-2) = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- **State** the final values:
  $k = 1$ or $k = 2$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: Find the eigenvalues and corresponding eigenvectors of the matrix $\mathbf{M} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$.
parts.markscheme:

- State the eigenvector for $\lambda = -1$: $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$ (or any scalar multiple) <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Show that if the system has two distinct real eigenvalues then $(a - d)^2 + 4bc > 0$.
parts.markscheme:
• The characteristic equation is given by:
    $$ \begin{vmatrix} a - \lambda & b \\ c & d - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = 0 \implies (a - \lambda)(d - \lambda) - bc = 0 \implies \lambda^2 - (a + d)\lambda + (ad - bc) = 0 $$ M1A1A1
  • Solving for $\lambda$:
    $$ \lambda = \frac{a + d \pm \sqrt{(a + d)^2 - 4(ad - bc)}}{2} $$
  • For two distinct real roots require:
    $$ (a + d)^2 - 4(ad - bc) > 0 $$ R1
  • Expanding:
    $$ \implies a^2 + 2ad + d^2 - 4ad + 4bc > 0 \implies a^2 - 2ad + d^2 + 4bc > 0 $$ A1A1
  • Simplifying:
    $$ \implies (a - d)^2 + 4bc > 0 $$ AG

6 marks total

    $$ a + d = 0 $$ and $$ (a - d)^2 + 4bc < 0 $$ R1A1A1
  • Simplify conditions:
    $$ \implies d = -a $$ and $$ \implies a^2 + bc < 0 $$ A1A1

5 marks total

parts.content:
In the case when there is a pair of purely imaginary eigenvalues you are told that the solution will form an ellipse. You are also told that the initial conditions are such that the ellipse is large enough that it will cross both the positive and negative $x$-axes and the positive and negative $y$-axes.

By considering the differential equations at these four crossing points investigate if the trajectory is in a clockwise or anticlockwise direction round the ellipse. Give your decision in terms of $b$ and $c$. Using Part 3 show that your conclusions are consistent.
parts.markscheme:
• When crossing the $x$-axis, $y = 0$ so $\dot{y} = cx$ M1A1
  • When crossing the positive $x$-axis, $\dot{y}$ has the sign of $c$. A1
  • When crossing the negative $x$-axis, $\dot{y}$ has the sign of $-c$. A1
  • Hence if $c$ is positive the trajectory is anticlockwise and if $c$ is negative the trajectory is clockwise. R1R1
  • When crossing the $y$-axis, $x = 0$ so $\dot{x} = by$ M1A1
  • When crossing the positive $y$-axis, $\dot{x}$ has the sign of $b$. A1
  • When crossing the negative $y$-axis, $\dot{x}$ has the sign of $-b$. A1
  • Hence if $b$ is positive the trajectory is clockwise and if $b$ is negative the trajectory is anticlockwise. R1R1
  • Since by Part 3, $b$ and $c$ have opposite signs the above conditions agree with each other. R1

13 marks total

steps.title: Analyze x-axis crossings
steps.content:
To determine the direction of the trajectory, we first look at where the ellipse crosses the $x$-axis. At any point on the $x$-axis, the $y$-coordinate is $0$.

Substituting $y = 0$ into our second differential equation:
$$\dot{y} = cx + d(0) = cx$$

This tells us the vertical component of the velocity at these intercepts depends only on $c$ and the position $x$.

highlights.text: cross both the positive and negative x-axes

steps.title: Determine rotation using c
steps.content:
Now, let's consider the specific crossing points on the $x$-axis:

*   **Positive $x$-axis ($x > 0$):** Here, $\dot{y}$ has the same sign as $c$. If $c > 0$, the trajectory is moving upwards (into Quadrant I). If $c < 0$, it is moving downwards (into Quadrant IV).
*   **Negative $x$-axis ($x < 0$):** Here, $\dot{y}$ has the opposite sign of $c$ (since $x$ is negative). If $c > 0$, the trajectory moves downwards.

Following the flow from the positive $x$-axis:
- If $c > 0$, moving up from the $x$-axis indicates an **anticlockwise** rotation.
- If $c < 0$, moving down from the $x$-axis indicates a **clockwise** rotation.

highlights.text: investigate if the trajectory is in a clockwise or anticlockwise direction

steps.title: Analyze y-axis crossings
steps.content:
Next, we look at the $y$-axis intercepts, where $x = 0$. Substituting this into our first differential equation:
$$\dot{x} = a(0) + by = by$$

This shows the horizontal component of the velocity at these intercepts depends only on $b$ and the position $y$.

steps.title: Determine rotation using b
steps.content:
Let's check the behavior at the $y$-axis crossing points:

*   **Positive $y$-axis ($y > 0$):** Here, $\dot{x}$ has the same sign as $b$. If $b > 0$, the trajectory moves to the right. If $b < 0$, it moves to the left.
*   **Negative $y$-axis ($y < 0$):** Here, $\dot{x}$ has the opposite sign of $b$.

Following the flow from the positive $y$-axis:
- If $b > 0$, moving right from the $y$-axis (into Quadrant I) indicates a **clockwise** rotation.
- If $b < 0$, moving left from the $y$-axis (into Quadrant II) indicates an **anticlockwise** rotation.

steps.title: Verify consistency with Part 3
steps.content:
From Part 3, we know that for a system to have purely imaginary eigenvalues (a center), the product of the off-diagonal elements must be negative ($bc < 0$ if $a=d=0$, or more generally $bc < ad$). This implies that **$b$ and $c$ must have opposite signs**.

Let's test the consistency:
- If **$c > 0$**, we found the rotation is **anticlockwise**. Since $b$ must have the opposite sign, **$b < 0$**, which also results in **anticlockwise** rotation.
- If **$c < 0$**, we found the rotation is **clockwise**. Since $b$ must be positive, **$b > 0$**, which also results in **clockwise** rotation.

Both conditions agree perfectly, confirming the analysis is consistent.

parts.content: Find the characteristic polynomial of the matrix $B$.
parts.markscheme:
- Characteristic polynomial = $\det(B - \lambda I)$ 

- Substitute matrix values: $B - \lambda I = \begin{pmatrix} -\lambda & 1 \\ -2 & -3-\lambda \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate determinant: $\det(B - \lambda I) = (-\lambda)(-3-\lambda) - (-2)(1)$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Simplify expression: $= \lambda^2 + 3\lambda + 2$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Award full marks for correct final answer with clear working shown</note>

parts.content: Find the eigenvalues of the matrix $B$.
parts.markscheme:
- State characteristic equation: $\lambda^2 + 3\lambda + 2 = 0$ 

- Solve for first eigenvalue: $\lambda = -1$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Solve for second eigenvalue: $\lambda = -2$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Therefore eigenvalues of matrix $B$ are $\lambda_1 = -1$ and $\lambda_2 = -2$ 

2 marks total

<note>Award full marks for correct eigenvalues with clear working shown</note>

parts.content: Find the eigenvectors corresponding to each eigenvalue of the matrix $B$.
parts.markscheme:
- For $\lambda = -1$:
  - Solving $(B + I)\mathbf{v} = 0$ where $B + I = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -2 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>M1</marks>
  
  - From $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ -2 & -2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$, get $x = -y$ 
  
  - First eigenvector is $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- For $\lambda = -2$:
  - Solving $(B + 2I)\mathbf{v} = 0$ where $B + 2I = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -2 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>M1</marks>
  
  - From $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ -2 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$, get $x = -0.5y$ 
  
  - Second eigenvector is $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Any scalar multiple of these eigenvectors is also valid</note>

parts.content: Determine if the matrix $B$ is diagonalizable.
parts.markscheme:
- Check if eigenvectors $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix}$ and $\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}$ are linearly independent 

- Set up linear combination: $c_1\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} + c_2\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$

- Solve system of equations:
  $$ \begin{cases} c_1 + c_2 = 0 \\ -c_1 - 2c_2 = 0 \end{cases} $$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Since this system has only trivial solution ($c_1 = c_2 = 0$), the eigenvectors are linearly independent 

- Since we have 2 linearly independent eigenvectors for a $2 \times 2$ matrix, $B$ is diagonalizable <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Full marks require both verification of linear independence and correct conclusion about diagonalizability</note>

parts.content: Find the eigenvalues of matrix $A$.
parts.markscheme:
- Set the characteristic equation $\det(A - \lambda I) = 0$:
$$\begin{vmatrix} 3 - \lambda & 2 \\ -1 & 6 - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = 0$$
- Expand the determinant to form the characteristic polynomial:
$(3 - \lambda)(6 - \lambda) + 2 = 0$
$\lambda^2 - 9\lambda + 20 = 0$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Solve for the eigenvalues:
$(\lambda - 4)(\lambda - 5) = 0$
$\lambda = 4$ or $\lambda = 5$ <marks>A1 A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Find the corresponding eigenvectors.
parts.markscheme:
- For $\lambda = 4$, substitute into the system $(A - 4I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$:
$$\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 2 \\ -1 & 2 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$
- Solve for the relationship between $x$ and $y$:
$-x + 2y = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{x}{y} = \frac{2}{1}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- State the first eigenvector:
$\mathbf{v}_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$ (or any multiple) <marks>A1</marks>
- For $\lambda = 5$, substitute into the system $(A - 5I)\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$:
$$\begin{pmatrix} -2 & 2 \\ -1 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$
- Solve for the relationship between $x$ and $y$:
$-x + y = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{x}{y} = 1$ <marks>A1</marks>
- State the second eigenvector:
$\mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}$ (or any multiple) <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

with general solution:

$$

$$

parts.content: Verify that $$\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} t \\ -t+1 \end{pmatrix}e^{2t}$$ is also a solution.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute $x = te^{2t}$ into $\frac{dx}{dt}$: $\frac{dx}{dt} = 1e^{2t} + 2te^{2t} = (1+2t)e^{2t}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute into RHS: $3x + y = 3te^{2t} + (-t+1)e^{2t} = (2t+1)e^{2t}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- LHS = RHS verified for first equation <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute $y = (-t+1)e^{2t}$ into $\frac{dy}{dt}$: $\frac{dy}{dt} = -1e^{2t} + 2(-t+1)e^{2t} = (1-2t)e^{2t}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute into RHS: $-x + y = -te^{2t} + (-t+1)e^{2t} = (1-2t)e^{2t}$ (matches LHS) <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the components
steps.content:
From the general solution provided, we can separate the matrix equation into two individual equations for $x$ and $y$. 

Using the constants $A = 0$ and $B = 1$ (determined in the previous parts of the question), the equations are:

$$\begin{aligned} x &= Ae^{2t} + Bte^{2t} \\ y &= -Ae^{2t} + B(-t+1)e^{2t} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting our constants, these simplify to:

$$\begin{aligned} x &= (1)te^{2t} \\ y &= (1)(-t+1)e^{2t} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate x at t=2
steps.content:
To find the value of $x$ when $t = 2$, we substitute $t = 2$ into the equation for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} x &= (2)e^{2(2)} \\ x &= 2e^4 \end{aligned}$$

This earns the first mark.

steps.title: Calculate y at t=2
steps.content:
Now, we substitute $t = 2$ into the equation for $y$:

$$\begin{aligned} y &= (-2 + 1)e^{2(2)} \\ y &= (-1)e^4 \\ y &= -e^4 \end{aligned}$$

This earns the second mark. These results provide the specific coordinates of the system's state at that exact time.

parts.content: Find the equation of the asymptote of the trajectory as $t \to \infty$.
parts.markscheme:
- As $t \to \infty$, the terms with $t$ dominate: $x \approx Bte^{2t}$ and $y \approx -Bte^{2t}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- The ratio $\frac{y}{x} \to \frac{-t}{t} = -1$ as $t \to \infty$ <marks>A1</marks>

- The asymptote is $y = -x$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content:
Find the particular solution given the initial conditions:

At $t = 0$:
- $x = 20$
- $y = 10$
parts.markscheme:
- At $t = 0$:

- First equation: $20 = A(1) + B(0) \Rightarrow A = 20$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Second equation: $10 = -A + B(1) \Rightarrow 10 = -20 + B$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Solving for B: $B = 30$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Substitute constants into general solution <marks>M1</marks>

- Particular solution: $$\begin{pmatrix}x\\y\end{pmatrix} = 20\begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\end{pmatrix}e^{2t} + 30\begin{pmatrix}t\\-t+1\end{pmatrix}e^{2t}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

parts.content:
Verify that 

$$\begin{pmatrix}x\\y\end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\end{pmatrix}e^{2t}$$ 

is a solution to the system.
parts.markscheme:
- Let $\vec{v} = \begin{pmatrix}1\\-1\end{pmatrix}e^{2t}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Verify second equation:
  LHS = $-2e^{2t}$

parts.content: Find the inverse of the matrix $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$.
parts.markscheme:
Method 1: Using adjoint method

  • Calculate the determinant and cofactors: M1 $$ |A| = 1(4 - 2) - 2(4 - 4) + 1(1 - 2) = 1 $$ $$ C = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 & -1 \\ -7 & 2 & 3 \\ 3 & -1 & -1 \end{pmatrix} $$
  • State the inverse matrix (transpose of cofactor matrix divided by determinant): A1 $$ A^{-1} = \frac{1}{|A|} C^T = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -7 & 3 \\ 0 & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix} $$

2 marks total

parts.content:
Hence solve the system of equations

$$ \begin{aligned} x + 2y + z &= 0 \\ x + y + 2z &= 7 \\ 2x + y + 4z &= 17 \end{aligned} $$
parts.markscheme:
• Write the system in matrix form: M1 $$ \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 4 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 7 \\ 17 \end{pmatrix} $$
  • Use the inverse from part (a): M1 $$ \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -7 & 3 \\ 0 & 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 3 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 7 \\ 17 \end{pmatrix} $$
  • Multiply matrices and solve: M1 $$ \begin{aligned} x &= 2(0) - 7(7) + 3(17) = 2 \\ y &= 0(0) + 2(7) - 1(17) = -3 \\ z &= -1(0) + 3(7) - 1(17) = 4 \end{aligned} $$
  • State the final answer: A1 $$ x = 2, \quad y = -3, \quad z = 4 $$

4 marks total

parts.content: Given that $A = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & -2 \\ -3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$ and $I = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$, find the values of $\lambda$ for which $(A - \lambda I)$ is a singular matrix.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** the condition for a singular matrix: a matrix is singular if its determinant is zero.

- **Attempt** to form the matrix $(A - \lambda I)$ by subtracting $\lambda$ from the diagonal elements:
  $A - \lambda I = \begin{pmatrix} 3 - \lambda & -2 \\ -3 & 4 - \lambda \end{pmatrix}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- **Calculate** the determinant of $(A - \lambda I)$:
  $\det(A - \lambda I) = (3 - \lambda)(4 - \lambda) - (-2)(-3)$ <marks>M1</marks>

- **Simplify** the determinant expression:
  $(12 - 3\lambda - 4\lambda + \lambda^2) - 6 = \lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 6$ <marks>A1</marks>

- **Set** the determinant to zero and solve for $\lambda$:
  $\lambda^2 - 7\lambda + 6 = 0 \Rightarrow (\lambda - 6)(\lambda - 1) = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- **State** the final values:
  $\lambda = 1, \lambda = 6$ <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

parts.content: Show that the eigenvalues of $\boldsymbol{A}$ are real if $(a - d)^2 + 4bc \geqslant 0$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the characteristic equation:
  $\begin{vmatrix} a - \lambda & b \\ c & d - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Expand the determinant:
  $(a - \lambda)(d - \lambda) - bc = 0$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Simplify to quadratic form:
  $\lambda^2 - (a + d)\lambda + ad - bc = 0$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State condition for real roots (discriminant $\geqslant 0$):
  $(a + d)^2 - 4(ad - bc) \geqslant 0$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Simplify to obtain the required inequality:
  $(a - d)^2 + 4bc \geqslant 0$ <marks>AG</marks>

steps.title: Set up characteristic equation
steps.content:
To find the eigenvalues ($\lambda$) of a matrix, we solve the characteristic equation. This is found by setting the determinant of $(\boldsymbol{A} - \lambda \boldsymbol{I})$ to zero, where $\boldsymbol{I}$ is the identity matrix.

$$\det(\boldsymbol{A} - \lambda \boldsymbol{I}) = \begin{vmatrix} a - \lambda & b \\ c & d - \lambda \end{vmatrix} = 0$$

highlights.text: matrix $\boldsymbol{A}$ is given by $\boldsymbol{A} = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$

steps.title: Expand the 2x2 determinant
steps.content:
We calculate the determinant of a $2\times2$ matrix by multiplying the elements on the main diagonal and subtracting the product of the elements on the other diagonal:

$$(a - \lambda)(d - \lambda) - (b)(c) = 0$$

Now, let's expand these brackets:

$$ad - a\lambda - d\lambda + \lambda^2 - bc = 0$$

steps.title: Form the quadratic equation
steps.content:
To solve for $\lambda$, we rearrange the expression into the standard quadratic form $A\lambda^2 + B\lambda + C = 0$:

$$\lambda^2 - (a + d)\lambda + (ad - bc) = 0$$

In this quadratic equation:
- The coefficient of $\lambda^2$ is $1$.
- The coefficient of $\lambda$ is $-(a + d)$.
- The constant term is $(ad - bc)$.

This is a standard topic in **AHL 1.15: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors**.

steps.title: Apply discriminant for real roots
steps.content:
For the eigenvalues $\lambda$ to be real numbers, the **discriminant** ($\Delta$) of our quadratic equation must be greater than or equal to zero ($b^2 - 4ac \geqslant 0$).

Substituting our coefficients into the discriminant formula:

$$\Delta = [-(a + d)]^2 - 4(1)(ad - bc) \geqslant 0$$

steps.title: Simplify to required form
steps.content:
Finally, we expand and simplify the discriminant to reach the required inequality:

$$\begin{aligned} (a + d)^2 - 4(ad - bc) &\geqslant 0 \\ a^2 + 2ad + d^2 - 4ad + 4bc &\geqslant 0 \\ a^2 - 2ad + d^2 + 4bc &\geqslant 0 \end{aligned}$$

Recognizing that $a^2 - 2ad + d^2$ is the expansion of $(a - d)^2$, we get:

$$(a - d)^2 + 4bc \geqslant 0$$

This completes the proof.

highlights.text: Show that the eigenvalues of $\boldsymbol{A}$ are real if $(a - d)^2 + 4bc \geqslant 0$

steps.title: Define Trace and Determinant
steps.content:
For a $2 \times 2$ matrix, we can use a handy shortcut to find the characteristic equation. First, let's identify the **trace** and the **determinant** of matrix $\boldsymbol{A}$.

The trace, $\text{Tr}(\boldsymbol{A})$, is the sum of the elements on the main diagonal:
$$\text{Tr}(\boldsymbol{A}) = a + d$$

The determinant, $\det(\boldsymbol{A})$, for this matrix is:
$$\det(\boldsymbol{A}) = ad - bc$$

steps.title: State Characteristic Equation Identity
steps.content:
The characteristic equation for any $2 \times 2$ matrix can be written directly using the identity:
$$\lambda^2 - \text{Tr}(\boldsymbol{A})\lambda + \det(\boldsymbol{A}) = 0$$

Substituting our expressions for trace and determinant, we get:
$$\lambda^2 - (a + d)\lambda + (ad - bc) = 0$$

steps.title: Apply the Discriminant Condition
steps.content:
The eigenvalues $\lambda$ are the roots of this quadratic equation. For these roots to be **real**, the discriminant (often denoted by $\Delta$) must be greater than or equal to zero.

Recall the discriminant formula for $Ax^2 + Bx + C = 0$ is $\Delta = B^2 - 4AC$. Here:
- $A = 1$
- $B = -(a + d)$
- $C = (ad - bc)$

So, for real eigenvalues:
$$\Delta = [-(a + d)]^2 - 4(1)(ad - bc) \geqslant 0$$

steps.title: Simplify the Inequality
steps.content:
Now we just need to expand and simplify the expression to reach the required form:

$$\begin{aligned} (a + d)^2 - 4(ad - bc) &\geqslant 0 \\ a^2 + 2ad + d^2 - 4ad + 4bc &\geqslant 0 \\ a^2 - 2ad + d^2 + 4bc &\geqslant 0 \end{aligned}$$

Notice that $a^2 - 2ad + d^2$ is a perfect square, $(a - d)^2$. Substituting this back in gives us the final result:
$$(a - d)^2 + 4bc \geqslant 0$$

- Reason that square terms are non-negative:

- Expand the determinant: $(a-\lambda)(b-\lambda) - (1-b)(1-a) = 0$ <marks>M1</marks> <marks>A1</marks>
- Simplify the equation: $\lambda^2 - (a+b)\lambda + a + b - 1 = 0$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Find the steady state probability vector for $\mathbf{M}$ in terms of $a$ and $b$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the steady state equation: $\mathbf{M}\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{p}$ with $\mathbf{p} = \begin{pmatrix} p \\ 1-p \end{pmatrix}$
- Substitute the matrix and vector:
  $$ \begin{pmatrix} a & 1-b \\ 1-a & b \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} p \\ 1-p \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} p \\ 1-p \end{pmatrix} $$
- Form the algebraic equation: $ap + (1-b)(1-p) = p \implies ap + 1 - b - p + bp = p$ <marks>M1</marks> <marks>A1</marks>
- Simplify to solve for $p$: $1 - b = (2 - a - b)p$
- Solve for $p$: $p = \frac{1-b}{2-a-b}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the second component: $1-p = \frac{1-a}{2-a-b}$
- State the final vector:
  $$ \begin{pmatrix} \frac{1-b}{2-a-b} \\ \frac{1-a}{2-a-b} \end{pmatrix} $$ <marks>A1</marks> <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

questions.specification:
A logistics company uses a discrete-time model to track the movement of shipping containers between two regional hubs, Hub A and Hub B. Each day, containers are redistributed between the two locations.

The state of the system is modeled by the equation
$$\begin{pmatrix} A_{n+1} \\ B_{n+1} \end{pmatrix} = \mathbf{T} \begin{pmatrix} A_n \\ B_n \end{pmatrix}$$
where $A_n$ and $B_n$ represent the number of containers at Hub A and Hub B respectively on day $n$, for $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$

steps.title: Recall the characteristic equation
steps.content: To solve this part, we start with the matrix equation we found in Part 2. This equation relates the matrix $A$, its square $A^2$, and the identity matrix $I$.

steps.title: Isolate the 5I term
steps.content:
Our goal is to express $I$ in terms of $A$. First, let's isolate the term $5I$ by moving the other terms to the right-hand side of the equation. We do this by adding $6A$ and subtracting $A^2$ from both sides:

$$5I = 6A - A^2$$

steps.title: Factor out matrix A
steps.content:
Now, we need to factor out the matrix $A$ from the right-hand side. 

Remember that in matrix algebra, $A$ is equivalent to $AI$. So, we can think of $6A$ as $6AI$. Factoring out $A$ to the left gives us:

$$5I = A(6I - A)$$

You can double-check this by distributing $A$ back into the parentheses: $A \times 6I = 6A$ and $A \times A = A^2$.

steps.title: Divide by the scalar
steps.content:
Finally, to get $I$ by itself, we divide the entire equation by the scalar $5$ (which is the same as multiplying by $\frac{1}{5}$):

$$I = \frac{1}{5}A(6I - A)$$

This matches the required form perfectly!

steps.title: Start with the RHS
steps.content: To show that the identity holds, we will start with the expression on the right-hand side (RHS) and expand it. We want to show that this simplifies down to the identity matrix $I$.

steps.title: Expand the expression
steps.content:
Distribute the matrix $A$ across the terms in the parentheses. Remember that multiplying any matrix by the identity matrix $I$ results in the original matrix ($AI = A$).

$$
\begin{aligned} \text{RHS} &= \frac{1}{5}A(6I - A) \\
&= \frac{1}{5}(6AI - A^2) \\
&= \frac{1}{5}(6A - A^2) \end{aligned}
$$

steps.title: Isolate $A^2$
steps.content: From Part 2, we have the characteristic equation for matrix $A$. We can rearrange this to find an expression for $A^2$ to use in our substitution.

steps.title: Rearrange the equation
steps.content:
Isolate $A^2$ by moving the other terms to the right side of the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} A^2 - 6A + 5I &= O \\ A^2 &= 6A - 5I \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute and simplify
steps.content:
Now, substitute our expression for $A^2$ back into the expanded RHS from Step 1:

$$
\begin{aligned} \text{RHS} &= \frac{1}{5}(6A - A^2) \\
&= \frac{1}{5}(6A - (6A - 5I)) \\
&= \frac{1}{5}(6A - 6A + 5I) \\
&= \frac{1}{5}(5I) \\
&= I \end{aligned}
$$

Since the RHS simplifies exactly to $I$, we have shown that $I = \frac{1}{5}A(6I - A)$.

parts.content: Use the result from Part 3 to explain why $A$ is non-singular.
parts.markscheme:
METHOD 1

  • Rearrange the identity: $I = \frac{1}{5}A(6I - A) = A \left[ \frac{1}{5}(6I - A) \right]$ M1
  • State that by definition $\frac{1}{5}(6I - A)$ is the inverse of $A$ R1
  • Conclude that since $A^{-1}$ exists, $A$ is non-singular R1

METHOD 2

  • State that $\det(I) = 1 (\neq 0)$ R1
  • Apply determinant to both sides: $\det \left( \frac{1}{5}A(6I - A) \right) = \det(A) \times \det \left( \frac{1}{5}(6I - A) \right) \neq 0$ M1
  • Conclude that $\det(A) \neq 0$ and so $A$ is non-singular R1

3 marks total

steps.title: Start with the Part 3 result
steps.content:
From Part 3, we found the matrix equation that relates $A$, $A^2$, and the identity matrix $I$:

$$A^2 - 6A + 5I = O$$

To show that $A$ is non-singular using the **Matrix Inverse Definition** approach, we need to prove that an inverse matrix $A^{-1}$ exists such that $AA^{-1} = I$.

steps.title: Isolate the identity matrix
steps.content:
We rearrange the equation to get the term with the identity matrix $I$ on one side:

$$5I = 6A - A^2$$

By moving the terms involving $A$ to the right, we are setting up a structure where we can factor out $A$.

steps.title: Factor out matrix A
steps.content:
Now, we factor out $A$ from the right-hand side. Remember that when we factor $A$ out of $6A$, we are left with $6I$ because we must maintain matrix dimensions:

$$5I = A(6I - A)$$

Next, divide both sides by 5 to isolate $I$ completely:

$$I = A \left[ \frac{1}{5}(6I - A) \right]$$

steps.title: Define the inverse
steps.content:
The definition of an inverse matrix states that if there exists a matrix $B$ such that $AB = I$, then $B = A^{-1}$. 

Looking at our equation:

$$I = A \underbrace{\left[ \frac{1}{5}(6I - A) \right]}_{A^{-1}}$$

We have successfully identified that $A^{-1} = \frac{1}{5}(6I - A)$.

highlights.text: Using the inverse matrix, if it exists, to solve for a system of linear equations

steps.title: Conclude non-singularity
steps.content:
A matrix is defined as **non-singular** if its inverse exists. Since we have shown that $A^{-1}$ can be expressed in terms of $A$ and $I$, the inverse exists. 

Therefore, $A$ is non-singular.

steps.title: Recall the identity relationship
steps.content:
From Part 3, we established a relationship between the matrix $A$ and the identity matrix $I$. We found that:

$$I = \frac{1}{5}A(6I - A)$$

To show that $A$ is non-singular, we need to prove that its determinant is not zero: $\det(A) \neq 0$.

steps.title: Apply determinants to both sides
steps.content:
Let's take the determinant of both sides of our equation. We know that the determinant of the identity matrix $I$ is always $1$.

$$\det(I) = \det\left( A \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{5}(6I - A) \right] \right)$$

Since $\det(I) = 1$, the equation becomes:

$$1 = \det\left( A \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{5}(6I - A) \right] \right)$$

steps.title: Use the Determinant Product Rule
steps.content:
Now we use the **Determinant Product Rule**, which states that for any two square matrices $X$ and $Y$, $\det(XY) = \det(X) \cdot \det(Y)$. Applying this to our equation:

$$1 = \det(A) \times \det\left( \frac{1}{5}(6I - A) \right)$$

This shows that the product of the determinant of $A$ and the determinant of the other matrix must equal $1$.

highlights.text: Apply determinant to both sides: det ( 1/5 A(6I - A) ) = det(A) × det ( 1/5 (6I - A) ) ≠ 0

steps.title: Conclude that A is non-singular
steps.content:
For the product of two numbers to be $1$, neither of those numbers can be zero. 

$$\text{If } \det(A) \times \text{something} = 1, \text{ then } \det(A) \neq 0$$

By definition, a matrix is **non-singular** if its determinant is non-zero. Since $\det(A) \neq 0$, we have successfully explained why $A$ is non-singular.

highlights.text: Conclude that det(A) ≠ 0 and so A is non-singular

parts.content: Use the values from Part 2 to express $A^4$ in the form $pA + qI$ where $p, q \in \mathbb{Z}$.
parts.markscheme:
METHOD 1

  • Rearrange Part 2 result for $A^2$: $A^2 = 6A - 5I$ A1
  • Square the expression to find $A^4$: $A^4 = (6A - 5I)^2$ M1
  • Expand: $= 36A^2 - 60A + 25I$ A1
  • Substitute $A^2$ again: $= 36(6A - 5I) - 60A + 25I$ M1
  • Simplify to the final form: $= 156A - 155I$ ($p = 156, q = -155$) A1

METHOD 2

  • Rearrange Part 2 result for $A^2$: $A^2 = 6A - 5I$ A1
  • Multiply by $A$ to find $A^3$: $A^3 = 6A^2 - 5A$ where $A^2 = 6A - 5I$ M1
  • Simplify $A^3$: $= 31A - 30I$ A1
  • Multiply by $A$ to find $A^4$: $A^4 = 31A^2 - 30A$ where $A^2 = 6A - 5I$ M1
  • Simplify to the final form: $= 156A - 155I$ ($p = 156, q = -155$) A1

5 marks total

Do not accept methods that evaluate $A^4$ directly from $A$.

steps.title: Identify Part 2 values
steps.content:
In Part 2, we found the values for $m$ and $n$ in the equation $A^2 + mA + nI = O$. 

Based on that result, the relationship is:
$$A^2 - 6A + 5I = O$$

This is a great starting point because we can use this to express $A^2$ in terms of $A$ and $I$. If you want to review the basics of matrices, check out **Topic 1: Number and Algebra (AHL 1.14)**.

steps.title: Isolate $A^2$
steps.content:
To eventually find $A^4$, let's first rearrange the equation from Part 2 to solve for $A^2$:

$$\begin{aligned} A^2 - 6A + 5I &= O \\ A^2 &= 6A - 5I \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Square the expression
steps.content:
Now, we square both sides of the equation to find $A^4$. Since $A^4 = (A^2)^2$, we have:

$$A^4 = (6A - 5I)^2$$

steps.title: Expand the binomial
steps.content:
Next, we expand the squared expression just like we would with algebraic variables, remembering that $AI = A$, $IA = A$, and $I^2 = I$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A^4 &= (6A - 5I)^2 \\
&= 36A^2 - 30AI - 30IA + 25I^2 \\
&= 36A^2 - 60A + 25I
\end{aligned}
$$

steps.title: Substitute $A^2$ again
steps.content:
The question asks for the answer in terms of $A$ and $I$, but we still have an $A^2$ term. Let's substitute $A^2 = 6A - 5I$ back into our equation:

$$A^4 = 36(6A - 5I) - 60A + 25I$$

steps.title: Simplify and solve
steps.content:
Finally, we distribute and combine like terms to find $p$ and $q$:

$$
\begin{aligned} A^4 &= 216A - 180I - 60A + 25I \\
&= (216 - 60)A + (-180 + 25)I \\
&= 156A - 155I \end{aligned}
$$

So, $p = 156$ and $q = -155$.

steps.title: Recall the quadratic relationship
steps.content:
From the result in Part 2, we have the matrix equation $A^2 - 6A + 5I = O$. To use the successive multiplication method, we first need to express $A^2$ in terms of $A$ and $I$.

Rearranging the equation gives us:
$$A^2 = 6A - 5I$$

steps.title: Calculate $A^3$
steps.content:
To find $A^3$, we multiply both sides of our $A^2$ expression by the matrix $A$:

$$\begin{aligned} A^3 &= A(A^2) \\ &= A(6A - 5I) \\ &= 6A^2 - 5A \end{aligned}$$

Notice that we now have another $A^2$ term on the right side. We should replace this with the expression we found in Step 0.

steps.title: Simplify the expression for $A^3$
steps.content:
Substitute $A^2 = 6A - 5I$ into our equation for $A^3$:

$$\begin{aligned} A^3 &= 6(6A - 5I) - 5A \\ &= 36A - 30I - 5A \\ &= 31A - 30I \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate $A^4$
steps.content:
Now, we repeat the process. To find $A^4$, we multiply our simplified expression for $A^3$ by $A$:

$$\begin{aligned} A^4 &= A(A^3) \\ &= A(31A - 30I) \\ &= 31A^2 - 30A \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the final expression
steps.content:
One last time, we substitute $A^2 = 6A - 5I$ into our equation for $A^4$ and simplify:

$$
\begin{aligned} A^4 &= 31(6A - 5I) - 30A \\
&= 186A - 155I - 30A \\
&= 156A - 155I \end{aligned}
$$

Thus, $p = 156$ and $q = -155$.

parts.content:
By considering the determinant of a relevant matrix, show that the eigenvalues, $\lambda$, of $\boldsymbol{A}$ satisfy the equation

$$\lambda^2-\alpha\lambda+\beta=0$$

where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are functions of $a, b, c, d$ to be determined.
parts.markscheme:
• Calculate the determinant of $(\boldsymbol{A} - \lambda \boldsymbol{I})$: $\begin{vmatrix} a-\lambda & b \\ c & d-\lambda \end{vmatrix} = 0$ M1
  • Expand the determinant: $(a-\lambda)(d-\lambda) - bc = 0$ M1 A1
  • Simplify the equation: $\lambda^2 - (a+d)\lambda + ad - bc = 0$ A1
  • State the values of $\alpha$ and $\beta$: $\alpha = (a+d)$ and $\beta = ad - bc$

4 marks total

parts.content:
Verify that

$$\boldsymbol{A}^2-\alpha\boldsymbol{A}+\beta\boldsymbol{I}=\boldsymbol{0}$$
parts.markscheme:
• Calculate $\boldsymbol{A}^2$: $\boldsymbol{A}^2 = \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} a^2+bc & ab+bd \\ ac+cd & bc+d^2 \end{bmatrix}$ M1 A1
  • Substitute into the expression $\boldsymbol{A}^2 - (a+d)\boldsymbol{A} + (ad-bc)\boldsymbol{I}$: $$\begin{bmatrix} a^2+bc & ab+bd \\ ac+cd & bc+d^2 \end{bmatrix} - (a+d)\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix} + (ad-bc)\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ M1
  • Simplify the expression: $$= \begin{bmatrix} a^2+bc-a(a+d)+ad-bc & ab+bd-b(a+d) \\ ac+cd-c(a+d) & bc+d^2-d(a+d)+ad-bc \end{bmatrix}$$ A2
  • Conclude the result: $= \boldsymbol{0}$ AG

5 marks total

Award A1 A0 for a single error.

parts.content:
Assuming that $\boldsymbol{A}$ is non-singular, use the result in part 2 to show that

$$\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{\beta}(\alpha\boldsymbol{I}-\boldsymbol{A})$$
parts.markscheme:
• Multiply the equation $\boldsymbol{A}^2 - \alpha\boldsymbol{A} + \beta\boldsymbol{I} = \boldsymbol{0}$ by $\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}$: $\boldsymbol{A} - \alpha\boldsymbol{I} + \beta\boldsymbol{A}^{-1} = \boldsymbol{0}$ M1 A1
  • Rearrange to solve for $\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}$: $\beta\boldsymbol{A}^{-1} = \alpha\boldsymbol{I} - \boldsymbol{A} \implies \boldsymbol{A}^{-1} = \frac{1}{\beta}(\alpha\boldsymbol{I} - \boldsymbol{A})$ AG

2 marks total

steps.title: Start with the known equation
steps.content:
From Part 2, we established the characteristic equation for matrix $\boldsymbol{A}$:

$$\boldsymbol{A}^2 - \alpha\boldsymbol{A} + \beta\boldsymbol{I} = \boldsymbol{0}$$

where $\alpha$ is the trace and $\beta$ is the determinant of the matrix. We are told that $\boldsymbol{A}$ is non-singular, which means its inverse $\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}$ exists and $\beta \neq 0$.

steps.title: Multiply by the inverse matrix
steps.content:
To find an expression for $\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}$, we multiply the entire equation by $\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}$. Since matrix multiplication is distributive, we apply it to each term:

$$\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{A}^2 - \alpha\boldsymbol{A} + \beta\boldsymbol{I}) = \boldsymbol{A}^{-1}(\boldsymbol{0})$$

$$\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}\boldsymbol{A}^2 - \alpha\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}\boldsymbol{A} + \beta\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}\boldsymbol{I} = \boldsymbol{0}$$

steps.title: Simplify using matrix identities
steps.content:
Now we simplify each term using the fundamental properties of the identity matrix $\boldsymbol{I}$ and the inverse matrix $\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}$:

- $\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}\boldsymbol{A}^2 = (\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}\boldsymbol{A})\boldsymbol{A} = \boldsymbol{I}\boldsymbol{A} = \boldsymbol{A}$
- $\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}\boldsymbol{A} = \boldsymbol{I}$
- $\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}\boldsymbol{I} = \boldsymbol{A}^{-1}$

Substituting these back into our equation, we get:

$$\boldsymbol{A} - \alpha\boldsymbol{I} + \beta\boldsymbol{A}^{-1} = \boldsymbol{0}$$

steps.title: Isolate the inverse matrix
steps.content:
Finally, we rearrange the terms to solve for $\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}$. 

$$\begin{aligned} \beta\boldsymbol{A}^{-1} &= \alpha\boldsymbol{I} - \boldsymbol{A} \\ \boldsymbol{A}^{-1} &= \frac{1}{\beta}(\alpha\boldsymbol{I} - \boldsymbol{A}) \end{aligned}$$

This confirms the result given in the question. In an IB exam, showing the multiplication by $\boldsymbol{A}^{-1}$ earns the first mark, and correctly reaching the final expression earns the second mark.

steps.title: Recall the characteristic equation
steps.content:
From the result in Part 2, we have the matrix equation relating $\boldsymbol{A}$, $\alpha$, and $\beta$:

$$\boldsymbol{A}^2 - \alpha\boldsymbol{A} + \beta\boldsymbol{I} = \mathbf{0}$$

This is a specific form of the Cayley-Hamilton theorem for $2 \times 2$ matrices, which you can learn more about in **AHL 1.15: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors**.

steps.title: Isolate the identity term
steps.content:
To begin the rearrangement, we need to isolate the term containing the identity matrix, $\beta\boldsymbol{I}$, on one side of the equation. We do this by moving the other terms to the right-hand side:

$$\beta\boldsymbol{I} = \alpha\boldsymbol{A} - \boldsymbol{A}^2$$

steps.title: Factor out matrix A
steps.content:
Now, we factor out the matrix $\boldsymbol{A}$ from the right-hand side. Remember that when factoring a scalar term like $\alpha\boldsymbol{A}$, we must include the identity matrix $\boldsymbol{I}$ to keep the expression in matrix form:

$$\beta\boldsymbol{I} = \boldsymbol{A}(\alpha\boldsymbol{I} - \boldsymbol{A})$$

We are allowed to do this because matrix multiplication is distributive, and $\boldsymbol{A} \cdot \boldsymbol{I} = \boldsymbol{A}$.

steps.title: Identify the inverse
steps.content:
We are given that $\boldsymbol{A}$ is non-singular, meaning $\beta \neq 0$ and the inverse exists. By dividing both sides by the scalar $\beta$ (or multiplying by $\frac{1}{\beta}$), we get:

$$\boldsymbol{I} = \boldsymbol{A} \left[ \frac{1}{\beta}(\alpha\boldsymbol{I} - \boldsymbol{A}) \right]$$

By the definition of an inverse matrix found in **AHL 1.14**, if $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{B} = \boldsymbol{I}$, then $\boldsymbol{B} = \boldsymbol{A}^{-1}$. Therefore:

$$\boldsymbol{A}^{-1} = \frac{1}{\beta}(\alpha\boldsymbol{I} - \boldsymbol{A})$$

steps.title: Define the Verification Goal
steps.content:
To show that a specific expression is the inverse of matrix $\boldsymbol{A}$, we must demonstrate that multiplying $\boldsymbol{A}$ by that expression results in the identity matrix $\boldsymbol{I}$. 

We are checking if:
$$\boldsymbol{A} \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{\beta}(\alpha\boldsymbol{I} - \boldsymbol{A}) \right] = \boldsymbol{I}$$

steps.title: Recall the Part 2 Result
steps.content:
From the previous part of the question, we found the relationship between $\boldsymbol{A}^2$, $\boldsymbol{A}$, and $\boldsymbol{I}$ (known as the Cayley-Hamilton theorem for a $2 \times 2$ matrix):

$$\boldsymbol{A}^2 - \alpha\boldsymbol{A} + \beta\boldsymbol{I} = \boldsymbol{0}$$

This can be rearranged to isolate the term $\beta\boldsymbol{I}$:
$$\beta\boldsymbol{I} = \alpha\boldsymbol{A} - \boldsymbol{A}^2$$

steps.title: Expand the Verification Product
steps.content:
Now, let's multiply $\boldsymbol{A}$ by the proposed inverse expression. We can factor out the scalar $\frac{1}{\beta}$ and distribute the matrix $\boldsymbol{A}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \boldsymbol{A} \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{\beta}(\alpha\boldsymbol{I} - \boldsymbol{A}) \right] &= \frac{1}{\beta} (\alpha\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{I} - \boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{A}) \\ &= \frac{1}{\beta} (\alpha\boldsymbol{A} - \boldsymbol{A}^2) \end{aligned}$$

Note that $\boldsymbol{A}\boldsymbol{I} = \boldsymbol{A}$ because $\boldsymbol{I}$ is the identity matrix.

steps.title: Substitute and Simplify
steps.content:
We can now substitute the result from Step 1 ($ \alpha\boldsymbol{A} - \boldsymbol{A}^2 = \beta\boldsymbol{I}$) into our expanded expression:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{\beta} (\alpha\boldsymbol{A} - \boldsymbol{A}^2) &= \frac{1}{\beta} (\beta\boldsymbol{I}) \\ &= \boldsymbol{I} \end{aligned}$$

Since the product equals the identity matrix, we have verified that:
$$\boldsymbol{A}^{-1} = \frac{1}{\beta}(\alpha\boldsymbol{I} - \boldsymbol{A})$$

parts.content:
Using this value of $a$, find $M^{-1}$ and **hence** solve the system of equations:

$$\begin{aligned} -x + 2y &= -3 \\ 2x - y &= 3 \end{aligned}$$
parts.markscheme:
- Find the determinant of $M$ for $a = -1$:
$$\det(M) = (-1)(-1) - (2)(2) = -3$$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Determine the inverse matrix $M^{-1}$:
$$M^{-1} = -\frac{1}{3} \begin{pmatrix} -1 & -2 \\ -2 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Simplify the expression for $M^{-1}$:
$$M^{-1} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Express the system of equations in matrix form:
$$\begin{pmatrix} -1 & 2 \\ 2 & -1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -3 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Solve for the variables by pre-multiplying by $M^{-1}$:
$$\begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} -3 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- State the final values:
$$x = 1, y = -1$$ <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

<note>The solution must use matrices. Award no marks for solutions using other methods.</note>
